subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Rogue Planets
description: A fast breakdown of Rogue Planets for builders.

hook.text: Rogue planets drift through space without orbiting any star. They're alone in the dark between the stars.

buildup.text: They were likely ejected from their original systems by gravitational encounters with other planets.

discovery.text: Microlensing surveys suggest billions of rogue planets wander the Milky Way—possibly outnumbering stars.

twist.text: Some rogues could retain internal heat. A thick hydrogen atmosphere might trap enough warmth for liquid water underground.

climax.text: JWST found free-floating planet-mass objects in Orion—some in pairs, challenging formation models.

punchline.text: Billions of starless worlds drifting in darkness. Some might still be warm inside.

quiz.question: How do most rogue planets end up without a star?
options: Gravitational encounters eject them from their original planetary systems
They form independently in interstellar gas clouds without any star
Their host stars explode, leaving the planets behind
